yeah so first up we've already established "Team Unique: " is to be abolished so

When I get around to wheels, this will be
> After the wielder unleashes their Exalt, they gain a temporary +`X`% Crit. DMG bonus, and each of their cards in your hand have a `N`% chance to have their Arithmetica Cost reduced by 1.

the debate as to whether "until played or end of turn" should be assumed by default for cost reduction would simply be the difference of one word; "(...)Cost temporarily reduced by 1."
Temporary is not shortened to Temp. here as there's plenty enough space, and I'd prefer to reserve the shortening for when it's both needed and grammatically sound.

(i.e., whenever we do get around to removing it, it needs to be big bang'd on every wheel as a whole and not rolled out in waves)

content: so Titan's Blade reduces the cost of the card until the end of the turn, no matter how many times it's played, correct?
yes

so in that case it's then much easier to assume "until played or end of turn" to be the default, so that we can simply say:
> (...)and each of their cards in your hand have a `N`% chance to have their Arithmetica Cost reduced by 1 until the end of the turn.
oh ok
yeah that makes sense
the turn-long reduction is pretty rare
in fact i'm not aware of any instances of it besides specifically titan blade and gramona
which again just makes the a2 ruleset change so obviously targeted to nerf titan blade
like, i get it, but still!!!!
damn you astral reign

and in the case of things like Prepare, we would invert that logic:
> If retained or discarded at end of turn, this card's Arithmetica Cost reduces by 1 until played.
side note but i'd remove at end of turn
since prepare activating on discard is like, a key mechanic of corpo
really? I didn't know that.
so it's like Aftershock then?
yeah, prepare works on discard
you can toss out miryam's rouse with lily's posse for example
and it'll trigger the cost down

sick.
> Whenever this card is retained or discarded, its Arithmetica Cost reduces by 1 until played.
for corpo specifically it's important because her lightning's aftershock returns the card to top of deck on discard so you can trigger it multiple times per turn

that would be a very misleading description.
Because prepare means **any** discard at **any** moment procs the -1 cost.
And also the end of turn.
> On discard or at end of turn, reduce this card's cost by 1 until played.

[we already covered that, friend](https://discord.com/channels/1402537664619479100/1402941858606219294/1418479990755823627)
> Whenever this card is retained or discarded, its Arithmetica Cost reduces by 1 until played.
yeah, retained suck ass
retaining a card is very abstract & imprecise, plus makes this definition depend on another keyword definition

hard disagree.
Retain is currently used and understood as a verb to describe the action of a card not being discarded at end of turn.
I like the ability to lean on very common language within a game to shorthand other effects. Makes the game feel clean and well-put-together.
I'd argue it's very precise, unless I'm missing something obvious.
Well, hard disagree on that
keyword depends on keyword = bad
retain as a verb -> very mid, that's a "do not auto-discard" and nothing more, yet retain implies a much more general non-discard property when used as a verb
overall very misleading

I went to pull up a convenient example because I happen to be playing a Horla team right now but I guess Dredge made the happiness tooltip incorrect with his fix. Command cards *are retained*; they don't gain or have Retain.
they gain the retain property
okay but they literally don't though
If the card "Gained Retain", it would say "<:warn:1408311425952387122>Retain."
oh it doesn't show because it's a property continuously applied to all of them, not a 1-time addition of the keyword
again, retain as a verb sucks
heavily
if you generate command cards, they'll have the retain property too
correct, therefore we should use the term Retain to describe the hidden property of "this card will act as though it has Retain".
only if the effect that created them gave them <:warn:1408311425952387122>Retain. In which case, <:warn:1408311425952387122>Retain is affixed to the card.
If you don’t mind I’m gonna start a contribution log to keep track of the awakeners whose chats I’ve translated already

just look at your git logs if you've named your commits
(don't worry I'll be drilling this into you tomorrow)

anyways, maybe this is really stupidly nitpicky and my own choice of words earlier is to blame but
I should have described Retain as a "keyword action". as a word it is a verb no matter how it's used here, so I could agree that it sucks as a verb by saying we could pick a completely different word entirely, but like...

\**points to slay the spire*\*

Retain doesn't retain in all circumstances, it's just "not auto-discard at end of turn". The verb is way larger in scope than the keyword. Unnecessary confusion.
You chose to write `discard | EoT&(discard|!discard)` as `discard | EoT&!discard`, abbreviating `EoT&!discard` with a keyword, when I chose to write `discard | EoT` (*sure sure if you want we can say if applies on `discard ⊕ EoT` if you really want to say it's not gonna proc twice on end of turn discard, but that's common english*). Simply put, we don't need to introduce more complex propositions/concepts for something simple as end of turn.

Basically Void is a very distinct keyword from Exhaust, and I'd be down to actually have a third one that would be Void+Exhaust, since it's common.
Could be fleeting could be anything, and it would read as `=Void+Exhaust : card is destroyed after playing or at end of turn`
Still, I would be down to change "Void"'s name

cool. effectively completely irrelevant, as it's creating a card that inherently always has Void and never has Exhaust.

as I said I'm very (unfortunately) aware we can't imply Exhaust within Void. With that said, it would be a coding change to imply "Void+Exhaust" into a third keyword, so that's off the table for us entirely.

It is, however, entirely on the table, for us to replace <:warn:1408311425952387122>Void with <:warn:1408311425952387122>Fleeting wholesale, and choose to cut down the wording of effects that affix these terms to one of 3 variants:

some variation of
> Affix <:warn:1408311425952387122>Exhaust to (it).
and 
> (create) an <:warn:1408311425952387122>Exhaust copy of (...)   /   (create copies). They have <:warn:1408311425952387122>Exhaust.

some variation of 
> (it) becomes <:warn:1408311425952387122>Fleeting.
and
> (create) a Fleeting copy of (...)
both to describe an effect that results in a card with "<:warn:1408311425952387122>Exhaust. <:warn:1408311425952387122>Fleeting." *(not keywording the 2nd effect is intentional for reasons)*

and, of course, the goddamn
> At the start of each battle, affix <:warn:1408311425952387122>Fleeting to each Symptom card in your deck. (...)
*(also we would **__NOT__** use Destroy here as Destroy specifically means removed from the deck effective for the entire exploration)*
Oh and I just checked the mutation cards from chapter 7 already have this void but non-exhaust
Though some similar symptoms have "destroyed/removed on playing", effectively giving them exhaust without the keyword

Big preference for
> create copies. They have <:warn:1408311425952387122>Exhaust.
or
> Create copies with <:warn:1408311425952387122>Exhaust.

-# *(sorry I've been typing this up for an hour now)*
Okay, so we have new wording for <:strdown:1408311306058203236>STR▼ and Stealing STR. I think it could absolutely go a step further in English though.

First, it's a fact we have no choice but to use the word "Temporary" for effects that only last until end of turn. However, it's still really long and regular use of it makes walls of text hard to read.
I propose we treat this word the same way we treat "Critical"; that being, anyone who can understand "Crit." as being short for Critical Hit can and will understand that "Temp." is short for Temporary. 

Second, some testing has confirmed that "Stealing STR" is two effects in a trench coat; A base value `N` is provided ("Steal `N` STR"), then calculations are performed *separately* on two distinct actions: "Gain `N` <:str:1408311379932221450>STR", and "Inflict `N` <:strdown:1408311306058203236>STR▼". This fact means it's relevant for "<:down:1418450818490695710>Steal" to remain an out-of-battle keyword, and important for in-battle descriptions to clarify this split into two verbs.

With those together, I'd like to propose some changes.
1. All <:strdown:1408311306058203236>STR▼ effects should share verbs with all other debuffs; those being, if it's my decision, "inflict" and "suffer":
> Ramona Skill "Assault Thesis" in-battle description:
> Inflict `N` <:strdown:1408311306058203236>STR▼ on all enemies. Draw a card; accrue 35 Keyflare for each 1 Arithmetica Cost of the drawn card.

2. <:down:1418450818490695710>Steal tooltip should accurately describe the actions being taken:
> ||~~Gain an amount of Temp. <:str:1408311379932221450>STR, and inflict a proportional amount of Temp. <:strdown:1408311306058203236>STR▼.~~||
> Inflict an amount of Temp. <:strdown:1408311306058203236>STR▼, and gain a proportional amount of Temp. <:str:1408311379932221450>STR.
It's tricky to reference a relative value like this twice, so it's important we use a word that doesn't insinuate that the "amount" of <:str:1408311379932221450>STR gained and the "amount" of <:strdown:1408311306058203236>STR▼ inflicted is "equal". I find proportional to be sufficient.

3. "Steal" can be used, as it is currently, to describe the split processes as a single value on in-battle descriptions, but only if the split process has been previously described by the card:
> Tinct Skill "Requiem" in-battle description:
> Cleanse yourself of <:fragile:1408309194691575808>Fragile, <:weakness:1408309179919368202>Weakness, and <:vulnerable:1408309178442973284>Vulnerable. For each enemy, gain `N` Temp. <:str:1408311379932221450>STR and inflict `N` Temp. <:strdown:1408311306058203236>STR▼ on them. The next card played this turn that could trigger <:warn:1408311425952387122>Leap does so.
> <:warn:1408311425952387122>Leap: <:down:1418450818490695710>Steal `X` <:str:1408311379932221450>STR from the front-most enemy instead.

Thoughts?
